guest_role: CEO at MyDrive, advisor to EC1 Capital, mentor at Virgin StartUp
assertiveness_why: Harry asks prepared, topic-hopping questions and light follow-ups but never challenges or contests anything Scott says.
question_style: Mostly open, sequential questions moving through founder qualities, mentorship, angel investing, and a closing lightning round; Harry occasionally asks a brief one-word follow-up ('Why would you say that?') but does not push back, contest claims, or plant his own thesis.
tone: Warm, enthusiastic, and promotional in the intro (heavy praise of the guest's credentials), casual and matey in the interview itself, no self-deprecation.
depth: Largely surface-level, covering founder traits, mentorship-seeking tactics, angel/VC dynamics, and SEIS/EIS tax schemes without deep technical or strategic follow-up; the closest to technical is a brief exchange on in-car telematics and insurance data.
harry_quotes: How did you learn to code then at such a young age?
What's your role at EC1 and what are you looking for?
How big is big enough for you?
Do you think SEIS and EIS have really affected the investing environment?
learnings: Scott frames great founders as relentlessly resourceful, driven by dissatisfaction with the status quo, creative, and humble enough to keep learning.
He recommends using outside, unconflicted mentors rather than board members for candid feedback on both business and psychology.
Cold-emailing high-profile people works if the message is short and asks one clear, valuable question.
He sees UK/European angel investing as maturing thanks to SEIS/EIS tax incentives and increasing exits feeding capital back into the ecosystem.
He views friends-and-family funding as ethically fraught since most startups fail, so that money should be treated as a write-off from the start.
He believes automotive/logistics (telematics, autonomous vehicles, risk-based insurance data) is an underappreciated large tech opportunity.
He argues you shouldn't build a company with an exit as the primary goal — focus on solving a real problem and opportunities will follow.
notable: Very early (episode 40, May 2015) format still includes a long biographical intro and a scripted lightning round, with no ad reads at all.
